summary: Gruber responds to critics like Cory Doctorow, Alex Payne, and Mark Pilgrim who argue the iPad's closed nature will prevent a new generation of tinkerers and programmers. He counters by noting that closed computing devices have always existed alongside open ones — his own first computer was an Atari 2600, completely closed to user programming. He highlights a 13-year-old who built and is selling a real iPad app on launch day, and points out that web development on iPad is completely unrestricted. He concludes that while something is lost in Apple's closed model, something valuable is also gained.
key_points: The iPad's closed nature is not unprecedented — closed computing devices like the Atari 2600 coexisted with open ones like the Apple II, and kids still became programmers
A 13-year-old developer selling a real iPad app on day one undermines the argument that the platform kills entrepreneurism and wonder
The car analogy illustrates how consumer technology naturally evolves toward sealed, maintenance-free designs as it matures
Web apps on iPhone OS have no Apple-imposed restrictions, offering an open creative platform even within the closed ecosystem
The shift is a trade-off, not a pure loss — something important is lost but something new and valuable is gained
money_quotes: 40 years ago you could open the hood of your car and see and touch just about every component in there. And you had to, because many of those components required frequent maintenance. To properly own a car required, to some degree, that you understood how a car worked.

Today, you open the hood of your car and you see a big sealed block and a basin for the windshield washer fluid. You can buy a new car, drive it for years, and never once open the hood yourself. That's the iPad.

We also had a sense that our programs, the ones we wrote in BASIC, were not 'real' programs — the ones we bought (and, uh, sometimes didn't) with commercial games and word processors were not written using BASIC.
The iPad and iPhone are closed compared to personal computers, yes. But they are remarkably open compared to so many kinds of computing devices.
He's 13 years old and he has created and is selling an iPad app in the same store where companies like EA, Google, and even Apple itself distribute iPad apps.
Imagine a 13-year-old in 1978 who could produce and sell his own Atari 2600 cartridges.
Somehow I don't think young Mr. Kaplan sees the iPad as hurting his sense of wonder or entrepreneurism.
Something important and valuable is indeed being lost as Apple shifts to this model of computing. But it's a trade-off, because something new that is important and valuable has been gained.
key_insight: The iPad is not the death of tinkering — it's a different kind of openness, where a 13-year-old can build and sell a real app alongside major corporations, which is a form of empowerment the nostalgic open-hardware era never offered.
